title: Cat Claws Growing Into Paw Pads: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do when your cat's claws grow into their paw pads. Understand the urgency, symptoms of infection, and how to get help.
content_html:
TL;DR: An ingrown cat claw occurs when a nail grows long enough to pierce the paw pad, causing significant pain and requiring veterinary attention within 24–48 hours to prevent infection.

What is an ingrown cat claw and why does it happen?

When a cat is unable to naturally wear down their claws through scratching, the nails can grow excessively long and curve back into the paw pads. This is particularly common in senior cats or those with mobility issues. Unlike human nails, cat claws grow in layers; when these layers aren't shed, the nail thickens and forms a sharp hook that can eventually pierce the sensitive tissue of the paw, leading to pain, inflammation, and potential infection.

Is an ingrown claw considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for an ingrown claw is Medium; while rarely life-threatening, it is a significant welfare concern because it is extremely painful for your cat.
  • Seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours if you see blood, discharge, limping, or obsessive licking of the paw.
  • Do not attempt to pull the nail out if it is deeply embedded, as this can cause further trauma or bleeding.

How does sending a photo help triage my cat’s ingrown claw?

  • A clear, well-lit photograph allows a professional to see the depth of the nail penetration and check for signs of secondary infection like swelling or pus.
  • Photos help the clinic determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and how to prioritize the appointment.
  • Visuals allow the team to prepare specific equipment for potential treatments, such as a minor surgical extraction or wound debridement.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When a cat's claw grows into the paw pad, treatment involves removing the ingrown portion of the claw, which may require regional anesthesia due to pain. If present, any loose horn should be removed with pincers. Skillful claw trimming is essential to alleviate pressure on the affected area.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1086)

faqs.question: Can I trim the ingrown nail at home?
faqs.answer: If the nail has already punctured the skin, it is highly recommended to seek professional help. Attempting to cut an embedded nail at home can cause intense pain and may result in the nail breaking off inside the wound.

faqs.question: Why do senior cats get ingrown nails more often?
faqs.answer: Older cats often develop thicker, more brittle nails and may suffer from arthritis, which makes them less likely to use scratching posts to shed their outer nail sheaths.

faqs.question: What are the signs of infection in the paw pad?
faqs.answer: Look for significant redness, heat radiating from the paw, a foul odor, or yellowish discharge (pus) around the site where the nail enters the skin.
